title: Choosing and Training a Model
concept: Logistic Regression offers a clear, interpretable view of each feature's influence, while a Random Forest trades some transparency for higher accuracy and more robustness to messy, non-linear features — the tradeoff StreamBox weighs when picking its model.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does one-hot encoding turn a category like plan_type: "Standard" into?

options.text: A set of binary (0/1) columns, one per category
A single scaled number between 0 and 1

exercises.explain: Right! One-hot encoding creates a separate 0/1 column for each possible category.

cards.title: Features ready, now what?
cards.body: StreamBox's features are clean and engineered: tenure_months, one-hot plan columns, scaled watch hours. Now the team has to pick an algorithm to actually learn the pattern that separates customers who stay from customers who churn.

cards.title: Two classification algorithms, two tradeoffs
cards.body: Logistic Regression learns a simple, transparent weight for each feature. A Random Forest trains many decision trees on random slices of the data and lets them vote — usually more accurate, especially on messy, non-linear data.

cards.title: A glass box vs. a committee vote
cards.body: Logistic Regression is a glass box: you can see exactly how much each feature (like a payment failure) pushed the prediction toward "churn." A Random Forest is more like a committee of 100 experts, each looking at slightly different clues, then voting — harder to explain any single vote, but the group's answer is usually sharper.

cards.title: StreamBox runs both
cards.body: The team trains both models on the same features. Logistic Regression reaches 82% accuracy but assumes fairly straight-line relationships. A Random Forest of 100 trees reaches 89% accuracy and handles quirks — like a lopsided tenure distribution or a rare outlier — without needing everything perfectly scaled first.

cards.title: "Always pick the most accurate model" — not always
cards.body: You might assume the higher-accuracy model always wins. In reality, interpretability matters too — a bank denying a loan often must explain why. But for StreamBox's specific mix of messy, non-linear features, the Random Forest's accuracy and robustness outweigh the loss in transparency.

cards.title: Logistic Regression vs. Random Forest
cards.body: Why StreamBox leans toward the forest:

left.text:
Highly interpretable
Assumes linear patterns
82% accuracy here

right.text:
More robust to messy data
Captures non-linear patterns
89% accuracy here

exercises.prompt: Which model gives the clearest, most interpretable view of exactly how much each feature influenced a prediction?
exercises.explain: Correct! Logistic Regression assigns a single, readable weight to each feature.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A Random Forest tends to handle messy, non-linear feature relationships better than Logistic Regression.
exercises.explain: True! Averaging many decision trees lets a Random Forest capture non-linear patterns and quirks that a straight-line Logistic Regression model misses.

exercises.sentence: A Random Forest is an ensemble of many decision ___ that vote together on a prediction.
exercises.explain: A Random Forest trains many decision trees on random slices of data and combines their votes.

exercises.prompt: Which description matches a Random Forest, not Logistic Regression?

options.text: Trains 100 decision trees on random slices of data and combines their votes
Assigns one clear, readable weight to each individual feature
Draws a single straight decision boundary between classes

exercises.explain: Correct! Voting across many trees is the defining trait of a Random Forest.

exercises.prompt: StreamBox's Logistic Regression model reaches 82% accuracy and is easy to explain. Its Random Forest reaches 89% accuracy, handles the messy tenure and watch-hour features well, but is harder to explain feature-by-feature. Given StreamBox mainly wants to correctly flag at-risk customers for a discount campaign (not satisfy a regulator), which should they choose, and why?

options.text: Random Forest — the higher accuracy and robustness to messy, non-linear features outweigh the loss of easy interpretability for this use case.
Logistic Regression — interpretability always matters more than accuracy, in every situation.
Neither — StreamBox should collect entirely new data before training anything.
Random Forest — because it is always the correct choice for every classification problem.

exercises.explain: Exactly! When the priority is catching at-risk customers accurately and the data is messy and non-linear, Random Forest's accuracy and robustness win out. Next: it's time to check how well the trained model actually performs!
